subject: ap-calculus-ab
title: Rate & Accumulation (Riemann Sums)
stimulus: A metal wire of length 8 centimeters (cm) is heated at one end. The table above gives selected values of the temperature $T(x)$, in degrees Celsius, of the wire at selected points $x$ cm from the heated end.

parts.prompt: Estimate $T'(7)$. Show the work that leads to your answer. Indicate units of measure.

scoring_points.description: Correct estimate of $T'(7)$ with supporting work and units

required_elements: Difference quotient using table values at $x=6$ and $x=8$

parts.prompt: Write an integral expression in terms of $T(x)$ for the average temperature of the wire. Estimate the average temperature of the wire using a trapezoidal sum with the four subintervals indicated by the data in the table. Indicate units of measure.

scoring_points.description: Correct integral expression for average temperature

required_elements: Four trapezoids using subintervals from table

scoring_points.description: Correct average temperature answer with units

alternatives.correct_example: $\approx 175.6875/8 \approx 175.688/8$ ... actually the trapezoid sum $A \approx 1405.5$, average $= A/8 \approx 175.688$ °C

common_traps: Forgetting to divide by the length 8

parts.prompt: Find $\int_0^8 T'(x)\, dx$, and indicate units of measure. Explain the meaning of $\int_0^8 T'(x)\, dx$ in terms of the temperature of the wire.

scoring_points.description: Correct value of $\int_0^8 T'(x)\, dx$ with units

required_elements: States temperature drops/decreases by 45°C from one end to the other
alternatives.correct_example: The temperature drops $45°C$ from the heated end to the other end of the wire

parts.prompt: Are the data in the table consistent with the assertion that $T''(x) > 0$ for every $x$ in the interval $0 < x < 8$? Explain your answer.

scoring_points.description: Computes two slopes of secant lines to compare rates
Correct answer: No, with valid MVT-based explanation

required_elements: By MVT, $T'$ takes value $-5.75$ on $(1,5)$ and $-8$ on $(5,6)$
So $T'$ must decrease somewhere, meaning $T'' < 0$ somewhere
alternatives.correct_example: No. By MVT, $T'(c_1) = -5.75$ for some $c_1 \in (1,5)$ and $T'(c_2) = -8$ for some $c_2 \in (5,6)$. So $T'$ decreases, meaning $T'' < 0$ somewhere in $(c_1, c_2)$.
